Home / Burn Rate Calculator

Burn Rate Calculator

Analyze your monthly cash flow: break down costs by category, calculate runway, and see how cost-cutting extends your survival time.

$
$
$
$
$
$
$

How to Use This Tool

1
Enter your average monthly revenue (leave at 0 if pre-revenue).
2
Fill in your team costs — salaries, contractors, benefits.
3
Add up all SaaS subscriptions and cloud hosting as Infrastructure.
4
Include ad spend, content marketing, and tool subscriptions as Marketing.
5
Enter remaining costs (rent, legal, insurance, travel) as Operations.
6
Enter your current cash balance to calculate runway and run-out date.
7
Review the cost structure chart to see which category is eating the most cash.
8
Check the what-if scenario to see how cost-cutting extends your runway.
9
tools.solopreneur-burn-rate-calculator.how_to_use.8
10
tools.solopreneur-burn-rate-calculator.how_to_use.9
11
tools.solopreneur-burn-rate-calculator.how_to_use.10

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between gross burn and net burn?

Gross burn is total monthly operating expenses before revenue. Net burn = gross burn - monthly revenue. For example, if you spend $12K/month and earn $5K/month, gross burn is $12K, net burn is $7K. Track both — gross burn shows spending discipline, net burn shows how fast your bank account actually shrinks.

What is a healthy runway for an early-stage business?

18-24 months is the gold standard after a fundraise. For bootstrapped entrepreneurs, 6-12 months is healthy. Under 3 months is critical — you need to act immediately on either cost-cutting or revenue generation.

Which cost category typically matters most?

Team/personnel costs are almost always the largest expense (50-70% of burn). If you need to cut quickly, headcount is the biggest lever. For SaaS tools, audit subscriptions quarterly — most people are paying for tools they stopped using months ago.

Should I include one-time expenses in burn rate?

No. Burn rate should reflect recurring operating expenses, not one-time costs like equipment purchases or legal fees. Track one-time costs separately for a clearer picture of ongoing cash needs.

How often should I recalculate my burn rate?

Monthly, when you close your books. If your net burn is trending up month-over-month, investigate immediately — cost creep is easy to miss but expensive over time.

tools.solopreneur-burn-rate-calculator.faq.5.q

tools.solopreneur-burn-rate-calculator.faq.5.a

Results

Copy All
Example

🔥 Cash Flow Health Check

💸 Burn Summary • Gross Burn: $12,000/mo • Net Burn: $7,000/mo (Gross − Revenue) • Annual Burn: $84,000/yr • To break even: Need +$7,000/mo more revenue (or cut costs by same amount) ⏳ Runway • Current Cash: $50,000 • Remaining Runway: 7.1 months • Est. Cash Run-out: Jan 2027 • Assessment: 🟡 Manageable — 6–12 months. Plan ahead. 📈 Burn Multiple • Net New Revenue: $3,000/mo • Burn Multiple: 2.3× 🔴 • Insight: Burning much faster than you're adding revenue. Need to improve efficiency. 💀 Default Alive/Dead Status • Status: 🔴 Default Dead: under 12 months — need growth or funding to survive 📊 Cost Structure • Team 66.7% █████████████░░░░░░░ • Marketing 16.7% ███░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ • Operations 12.5% ██░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ • Infrastructure 4.2% █░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ 🔄 Cost-Cut Scenarios • Cut 10%: Save $1,200/mo — Net burn $5,800/mo — Runway 8.6 mo (+1.5 extra) • Cut 20%: Save $2,400/mo — Net burn $4,600/mo — Runway 10.9 mo (+3.7 extra) • Cut 30%: Save $3,600/mo — Net burn $3,400/mo — Runway 14.7 mo (+7.6 extra)
